dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: Liszka applies the pre-1290 terminus of the Southern Passion to its source, the Ministry and Passion; this is a terminus ante quem, not an exact date.

entities.description: Anonymous Middle English temporale narrative comprising 3,048 lines in septenary rhyming couplets, probably composed as a sequel to The Nativity of Mary and Christ and subsequently revised into the Southern Passion.
entities.label: The South English Ministry and Passion

rights.basis: The work was composed in the thirteenth century and its author is unknown.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval text

entities.description: A later Middle English revision of the Ministry and Passion that introduces closer Vulgate translation and additional expository material.
entities.label: The Southern Passion

entities.description: The distinct Middle English saint's life cataloged as DIMEV 4928 (NIMEV 3159). Horstmann quotes 58 lines beginning at his line 82 in the apparatus to Ministry and Passion relative line 761.
entities.label: Life of St Mary Magdalene (South English Legendary version)

rights.basis: DIMEV catalogs the anonymous Middle English work in medieval manuscript witnesses; this underlying historical text is distinct from Horstmann's editorial transcription and modern manuscript photographs.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval Life of St Mary Magdalene text quoted in the apparatus

entities.description: A fifteenth-century South English Legendary manuscript whose Pater Noster extract begins around mid-page on f. 270r/canvas 571 after the terminal St Silvester text and continues only through the upper portion of f. 270v/canvas 572. DIMEV maps it to Ministry and Passion lines 427-472 and calls it 52 lines although that inclusive range contains 46 line positions. The lower portion of f. 270v opens the next catalog item, 'Judas was a luþer bryd.' The extract does not overlap the Laud fragment.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.25 (James 605)

notes: 46-48 lines to a page.

next_steps: Email special.collections@joh.cam.ac.uk with shelfmark B.6 (28), requesting the whole manuscript if feasible and at minimum ff. 25r-69v.
Ask separately for image reuse and repository-publication terms.
openQuestions.question: Can St John's College supply or identify a complete digital facsimile of MS B.6 (28), and what fees and reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Consult Pickering's 1984 edition pp. 8-10 and its lineation table through controlled access.
Map every surviving Laud line against Pickering's full-work line numbers.
notes: Inclusive arithmetic across 874-2047 minus 272 yields 902 rather than 901, so at least one boundary or loss count is approximate or exclusive.
openQuestions.question: How should the official work-line boundaries 874-2047, the 272-line internal loss, and the reported 901 surviving lines be reconciled arithmetically?

next_steps: Consult Pickering's full 1984 edition and lineation table through controlled access.

Transcribe and align the passage from around mid-page on f. 270r/canvas 571, after the terminal St Silvester text, through the upper portion of f. 270v/canvas 572, stopping before the opening of contents item 117.
notes: Canvas 571 is shared with the terminal St Silvester text above item 116; canvas 572 is shared with item 117, so only its upper portion completes the extract before the next text begins below a large red initial.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV call the Trinity R.3.25 Pater Noster extract 52 lines while mapping it to Ministry and Passion lines 427-472, an inclusive range of 46 positions?

next_steps: Consult Pickering's full 1984 manuscript description and any newer St John's cataloging or paleographical study.
openQuestions.question: Should St John's B.6 be dated to the queried late fourteenth century or the early fifteenth century?

relations.subjectLabel: Bell and Couch, The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (2011)

reviewNotes: CME00148 is not cataloged as a generic Life of Jesus or as the separate Infancy of Jesus. Horstmann's Leben Jesu is an editorial title for the Laud fragment of The South English Ministry and Passion.
St John's B.6 is a parallel fuller witness, not an ancestor of Horstmann's target text. Trinity R.3.25 preserves a collateral extract outside the portion surviving in Laud.
The 58 Middle English lines nested in the note to target line 761 are a passage-scoped quotation of a different work from the same codex, not additional lines of the Ministry and Passion.
The South English Ministry and Passion is earlier than and was revised into the Southern Passion; the direction reverses an older view corrected by Pickering in 1984.
Witness dialect localizations and manuscript production places are not transferred to the abstract work's place of composition.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00148. It encodes Carl Horstmann's 1873 title page and printed pp. 29-69: his 901-line transcription of the incomplete Laud Misc. 108 witness of the anonymous 3,048-line South English Ministry and Passion, with 204 editorial footnotes and the closing manuscript note. One footnote to relative line 761 also transcribes a 58-line passage of the distinct South English Legendary Life of St Mary Magdalene from Laud ff. 191r-v (Horstmann pp. 61-62; PDF pp. 73-74). Horstmann's German introduction on pp. 1-28 is not encoded. The target Laud fragment occupies ff. 1r-10v and represents work lines 874-2047, with about 272 lines lost between ff. 1 and 2; the principal fuller witness is St John's College Cambridge MS B.6, ff. 35r-69v. Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.25 preserves a non-overlapping Pater Noster extract that begins around mid-page on f. 270r/canvas 571 after the terminal St Silvester text and continues only through the upper portion of f. 270v/canvas 572, but DIMEV's reported 52-line extent conflicts with its inclusive work-line range 427-472. Complete public facsimiles of the entire Laud and Trinity codices are cached and mapped to the target and supporting portions. No public full-image surrogate of St John's B.6 was located, so its reading-room and enquiry route is recorded. The target fragment, quoted related work, abstract work, manuscript witnesses, nineteenth-century edition, later full edition, digital encodings, facsimiles, related Southern Passion revision, and repository artifact remain distinct.

catalogSummary.title: The South English Ministry and Passion (Laud Misc. 108 Fragment)

catalogingSubject.label: The South English Ministry and Passion (Laud Misc. 108 Fragment)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The 901 surviving Middle English verse lines of the Laud Misc. 108 witness of the anonymous 3,048-line South English Ministry and Passion, as first printed by Carl Horstmann in 1873 and encoded in CME00148. The item includes Horstmann's title page, 204 editorial footnotes, and a closing note, but not his pp. 1-28 German introduction. One footnote to relative line 761 transcribes a 58-line passage from the distinct South English Legendary Life of St Mary Magdalene in the same codex, ff. 191r-v; that quoted paratext is modeled as a nested content part and related work. Its lines are included in the whole-item representation extent and separately scoped as quoted Middle English, but they do not change the intrinsic form or discovery facets of the cataloged Ministry and Passion fragment. The target fragment begins and ends imperfectly and has an internal lacuna of about 272 lines between manuscript ff. 1 and 2. The abstract work, fragmentary Laud witness, fuller St John's B.6 witness, collateral Trinity R.3.25 extract, related Magdalene quotation, scholarly editions, and digital representations remain distinct. Horstmann's editorial title Leben Jesu and title-page reference to Kindheit Jesu do not make this a generic Life of Christ or an encoding of the separate Infancy poem.

scope.description: Horstmann's printing of the 901 surviving Laud lines.

dateStatements.display: 1984: first full edition of the 3,048-line work

scope.description: First complete scholarly edition, using St John's B.6.

extent.description: Five modeled content parts (four top-level and one nested): one seven-paragraph edition title page; one LG containing 903 direct L elements, of which the opening and closing rows are dotted lacuna markers and 901 are verbal target-work lines; an apparatus of 204 NOTE elements; a zero-item nested part for the 58 L elements that quote the distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene inside one of those notes; and one TAILNOTE transcribing and explaining a Latin manuscript direction. The 207 item total is therefore 1 title page + 1 target fragment + 204 notes + 0 separately numbered quotation items + 1 closing note. Whole-item verse extent is 959 verbal lines (901 target-work lines + 58 quoted lines), while the two dotted display rows are excluded; the raw XML has 961 L elements. The quote's zero item count prevents double-counting its containing footnote, so the form totals remain two prose parts, one verse item, and one mixed apparatus. Forty-two PB elements comprise the title page plus printed body pp. 29-69; the complete two-page Magdalene note is serialized before the PB for printed p. 62.

formStatements.description: Septenary rhyming couplets; DIMEV classifies the verse pattern aa and form as two-line.

scope.description: The abstract Ministry and Passion and its represented Laud fragment.

notes: Pickering contrasts MP's comparatively casual gospel paraphrases with the Southern Passion reviser's closer Vulgate translations.

scope.description: The medieval verse text.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Quoted usage keeps this passage visible without treating it as intrinsic language evidence for the cataloged Ministry and Passion fragment.

scope.description: The 58-line passage of the distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene quoted inside Horstmann's footnote to target line 761.

notes: The target fragment's intrinsic extent is 901 surviving Laud/CME verbal lines, while the whole encoded-item extent is 959 verbal verse lines after adding the separately modeled 58-line quotation; neither count is the full 3,048-line work or the raw XML L count.
The raw 961 L total consists of 901 target-work lines, two dotted lacuna rows, and one 58-line Life of St Mary Magdalene quotation nested in the footnote to target line 761.
The Laud fragment corresponds to full-work positions 874-2047 but has a substantial internal physical loss after its relative line 90.

The Trinity R.3.25 extract supports the abstract work's transmission without overlapping CME00148's fragment, but its reported 52-line extent conflicts with the inclusive range 427-472 and remains unresolved.
The modern German title, German editorial prose, and brief Latin citations are described at the edition/paratext layer and excluded from work-language discovery facets.
The 58-line Magdalene quotation is linked to DIMEV 4928, Laud ff. 191r-v, Digital Bodleian canvases 397-398, and Horstmann printed pp. 61-62/PDF pp. 73-74.
The closing Latin direction records lost or formerly misbound Passion material and does not establish a second surviving content part.
The Southern Passion is modeled as a later revision of the Ministry and Passion, correcting the older reversed dependency.
No institution-affiliation assumption is made in describing the work or its research value.

subjects.label: Passion of Christ
notes: The Laud/CME fragment ends during Judas's arrangement for betrayal, before most of the complete work's Passion narrative.

scope.description: The abstract work represented only in part by CME00148.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English narrative of Christ's public ministry, Passion, Resurrection, and the disciples' later activity, comprising 3,048 lines in septenary rhyming couplets. It was probably designed as a sequel to The Nativity of Mary and Christ. The later Southern Passion carefully revises it, often replacing its freer gospel paraphrase with closer Vulgate translation and adding expository material. CME00148 preserves only the 901-line Laud fragment within Horstmann's editorial frame; one footnote quotes 58 lines of a distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene. That passage is excluded from the target work's discovery facets but modeled separately and included in the encoded item's representation extent.

The surviving Laud portion opens mid-episode with Jesus healing a deaf-mute, then recounts miracles, teachings and parables, the Transfiguration, Lazarus, the entry into Jerusalem, the cleansing of the Temple, and opposition from Jewish leaders. It breaks off while Judas considers the thirty pieces associated with betraying Jesus. Both boundaries are incomplete, and an internal loss follows the first folio.

CME00148 contains Horstmann's edition title page and printed pp. 29-69, including footnotes and the closing report of a Latin manuscript direction. It omits the 28-page German introduction. The title page names both Leben Jesu and Kindheit Jesu, but the encoded body contains only the first-part Ministry and Passion fragment; the separate Infancy poem is not encoded. The note to relative line 761 quotes 58 lines of the separate Life of St Mary Magdalene from Laud ff. 191r-v, so that passage is retained as identified edition paratext rather than merged into the target work.

assertion.notes: The qualifier prevents the 901-line CME item from being mistaken for Pickering's complete 3,048-line edition.

It was probably designed as a sequel to The Nativity of Mary and Christ. The later Southern Passion carefully revises it, often replacing its freer gospel paraphrase with closer Vulgate translation and adding expository material. CME00148 preserves only the 901-line Laud fragment within Horstmann's editorial frame; one footnote quotes 58 lines of a distinct Life of St Mary Magdalene. That passage is excluded from the target work's discovery facets but modeled separately and included in the encoded item's representation extent.
